title: 2 Kings 15
subtitle: The Parade of Kings — Rapid Decline

sections.header: Verses 1–22 — Azariah/Uzziah; Five Kings of Israel in Rapid Succession

panels.ctx: Azariah (Uzziah) of Judah reigns 52 years and does right — but is struck with leprosy and lives in quarantine while his son Jotham governs. Meanwhile in Israel, political chaos erupts: Zechariah (6 months, assassinated), Shallum (1 month, assassinated), Menahem (10 years, extracted brutal tribute to pay Assyria), Pekahiah (2 years, assassinated). Five kings in one chapter — the narrator accelerates through them because the northern kingdom is spiralling toward destruction. The Assyrian shadow falls: Tiglath-Pileser III (Pul) extracts a thousand talents of silver from Menahem.

cross.note: Chronicles explains Uzziah’s leprosy: he entered the temple to burn incense, usurping the priests’ role. Pride at the height of success brought judgment.
“In the year that King Uzziah died, I saw the Lord.” Isaiah’s call vision occurs at the transition this chapter describes.

notes.note: Menahem’s tribute to Assyria teaches that those who abandon God for political alliances always pay a higher price than faithfulness would have cost. Foreign protection is never free.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Pûl is an alternate name for Tiglath-Pileser III, used in Babylonian chronicles. The identification is confirmed by the Assyrian king list.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tiglath-Pileser III (biblical “Pul”) is well attested in Assyrian records. His annals mention Menahem of Samaria (Me-ni-hi-im-me Sa-me-ri-na-a-a) paying tribute. This is one of the clearest Assyrian confirmations of a biblical king.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The rapid succession of assassinated kings demonstrates that the northern monarchy has lost all legitimacy. When power can only be seized by murder, the institution itself is bankrupt. The system Jeroboam I created is consuming itself.

sections.header: Verses 23–38 — Pekah, Jotham; Assyria’s Shadow Falls

panels.ctx: Pekah seizes Israel’s throne through yet another assassination and reigns 20 years. During his reign Tiglath-Pileser conquers the northern territories — Gilead, Galilee, Naphtali — and deports the population. The northern kingdom is being dismembered piece by piece. Hoshea assassinates Pekah and takes the throne. In Judah, Jotham reigns faithfully but cannot stop the rot: “the high places were not removed.” The chapter ends with the Aramean-Israelite coalition forming against Judah — the Syro-Ephraimite crisis that will trigger Isaiah’s Immanuel prophecy.

cross.note: The Aramean-Israelite threat against Judah produces Isaiah’s most famous prophecy: “The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and will call him Immanuel.”
Chronicles confirms the Assyrian deportation of the Transjordanian tribes: Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.

notes.note: The coalition against Judah tests whether the Davidic king will trust God’s promise or seek human alliances. This is the same test every generation faces: faith or self-reliance.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The cities listed — Ijon, Abel Beth Maakah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor — trace a path through upper Galilee. The deportation policy was standard Assyrian practice: removing local populations and replacing them with deportees from elsewhere.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tiglath-Pileser III’s campaign against Israel is extensively documented in his own annals. He records the conquest of cities in Galilee and the deportation of populations to Assyria — exactly as described here. Archaeological evidence of destruction layers at Hazor, Dan, and Megiddo confirms the military campaign.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Assyrian deportations introduce the theme of exile that will dominate the rest of 2 Kings. The removal of populations is not merely military strategy but theological judgment: the people are removed from the land God gave them.

ppl.text: Azariah (Uzziah) of Judah reigns 52 years and does right — but is struck with leprosy and lives in quarantine while his son Jotham governs…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe did what was right in the eyes of the Lord, just as his father Amaziah had done.ESVAnd he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, according to all that his father Amaziah had done.NIVThe Lord afflicted the king with leprosy until the day he died, and he lived in a separate house. Jotham the king\u2019s son had charge of the palace and governed the people of the land.ESVAnd the LORD touched the king, so that he was a leper to the day of his death, and he lived in a separate house. And Jotham the king\u2019s son was over the household, governing the people of the land.
